Why don't we go and talk to the people and see what their problems are, see what their desires are, see what they want and get a really clear understanding of what these people want and such a clear understanding that we know it better than they do. And then once we understand what they want, why don't we figure out how to get them what they want? Why don't we craft an offer? Why don't we figure out some services or product or whatever to help people get that and then why don't we just sell it to them? And it's a lot easier. And this is where people go wrong. People come up with something in their own head and then they're just gobsmacked as to why people don't want it when it's all pretty obvious. You can't fight the market. You know, if you try and fight the market, you're always going to lose. If you swim against it, you will drown. And it's so important that you understand that. But if you ride it, you will have the time of your life. And I'm not kidding. You will have a seriously good time. When I fought the market, I had three failed businesses back to back. I had my job board failure. I had my office lunch delivery business failure. I had this loyalty card app for cafes failure. Bam, bam, bam. Just nonstop rejection, nonstop failure and lost all sorts of money. It spent 12 hours a day spinning my tires. I didn't get anywhere. And it's because I cooked up these ideas in my own head. I thought, what do people want? And I figured it out. And then I found out I was wrong because I'm not people. I selfishly looked in myself for what people wanted when it was as easy and going out and looking at people and talking to people and figuring out what they actually want. And then when I actually started going with the market instead of against it, I started making a fortune. And the more accurately I understood the market and their desires, the more money I actually made. And this is what it's all about. It's about picking a niche. It's about understanding that niche, understanding their current situation and their desired situation better than they do themselves. And here are some different consulting service delivery models. So once you know what your niche's current situation is and then what their desired situation is, we need to give them some advice to help them get there. But there's all sorts of different ways we can deliver that advice. And here's what they are. They're done for you. And this is when you actually deliver the services yourself. So, you know, if you're helping accountants get more customers, then you are doing this for them. Like, you're setting up their landing pages, you're setting up their Facebook ads, you're setting up everything for them and you're doing it for them. You're actually delivering the services yourself. Now, you could do this yourself or you could use staff or contractors to do the work for you. Like, you could use other people or you could outsource it, whatever. This is called done for you. This is when you're doing the work yourself. Then there's done with you. And this is when you're doing some of the work and giving the client some of the work. So you're kind of dividing it up. You're saying, hey, look, you're going to be doing this stuff. I'm going to be doing this stuff. And so, you know, this is a different way of delivering the services. So you might say, well, you're going to come up with, you know, this, all of the website copy. You're going to come up with all of the images and everything. You're going to come up with the copy for the ads. And you're going to build out the landing pages and stuff. And then we're going to be running the ad campaign management for you. All right, so you're kind of dividing it up. Done with you. It's you're doing some clients doing some. Then there's one-on-one coaching. So this is advising the client on what to do in a one-on-one setting. So, you know, you could be, you know, you could be going to visit the client in person, sitting down and having a conversation with them. And you're saying, look, here's what you're going to be doing. I want you to try this. I want you to try that. And then the client is going away from that meeting and doing all of it themselves. So you're not actually doing any of the work. You're actually advising them on what work they're going to do and then they're going to do it. And you could do one-on-one coaching in person. You could do it on Skype. You could do it on the phone. Doesn't matter. There's different ways to do it. But it's one-on-one. And instead of doing it, you're advising. Then there's group coaching. So this is advising a group of clients on what to do at the same time. So instead of doing it one-on-one, you could do it in groups. So you could sign up like 10 people, 20 people, have them all in one group, and then have calls. Like you could have a call every week and all these people could jump on this call and ask different questions. And it would kind of be like a group of people being coached at the same time. You know, it doesn't have to be done one-on-one. You can do it in groups because people have similarities with other people. Like if you're teaching a group of people like, you know, how to lose weight, they might get more benefit of being in a group than they might being by themselves because other group members might have a problem and they're like, oh, I've got that problem too. Or other group members might have figured out a solution to a problem and they're like, how do you figure that one out? You know, sometimes you can do things in groups. So this is when you advise a group of clients on what to do at the same time. And then there's online programs, which is when you create an online training like the one which you're doing right now, Consulting Accelerator, and then letting the clients learn themselves. So this is totally hands-off. You know, instead of doing the work for the person, they're doing all of the work themselves. And instead of advising the person one-on-one or advising the person in a group, you're advising them in a pre-recorded online training. And you're letting the client do it all themselves. Totally hands-off. So these are some different consulting service delivery models. And here are what I call the evolutionary stages of a consultant. So, you know, how do consultants evolve over time? Like, where do they start out? And, you know, what are the sort of stages? Like, how do they develop and evolve? And, you know, how do you go from doing done-for-you services, where you're like, you know, literally doing all of the work for your client to the point where you create an online program and the client's doing all of the work themselves? How do you go from one side to the other? These are the evolutionary stages of a consultant. So this is how it works. You start out with done-for-you, and then you go through the different stages and you end up evolving into online programs. The reason why you start with done-for-you is because it's the most desirable and it's the easiest to sell. So what most people want is they want someone to do all of the work for them. Like, if you have a problem and you want it fixed, most people want someone to do all of it for them. You're like, hey man, can you do all this for me? If someone's like, yeah, then you're like, awesome, but that's what most people want. They want someone to do all the work for them. And this is what makes it the easiest to sell, which is why it's the best place to get started. So if you're a consultant and you're just getting started, done-for-you is where you want to start because this is where you learn things. When you're doing the work for someone, you learn how it's done and you learn how to do it and all of that stuff. And it's the easiest to sell, which means it's the easiest way to start getting some clients and the easiest way to start making some money. However, it has trouble scaling. So it has some pros and some cons. The pros are it's desirable and it's easy to sell, but the cons are at a certain number of clients, you kind of get maxed out. Like, once you have, say, 30, 40, 50 done-for-you clients, because you're doing so much work, you can't really take on more clients. And so the done-for-you model sort of reaches its limits. It reaches its limits at a particular point. And that's when you evolve into, like, the done-with-you, which is when you kind of divvy up the work. You give the client some, you take some. That allows you to serve more clients. However, it makes the pitch not as desirable because on the other end of the spectrum is online programs. This is like the one which I'm teaching to you right now. So these are the least desirable and the hardest to sell. Why? Because the person has to do all of the work themselves, and they, you know, they have to go through and teach themselves too. So an online program is the least desirable and it's the hardest to sell, but the pro of it, the positive of it is that it can really scale.
